days.headline: A novelty start at the Magny-Cours motor-racing circuit then east across open Burgundy farmland to Chalon-sur-Saône — another day for the sprinters, but open, exposed roads mean crosswinds could split the bunch.

picks.why: Won the Bordeaux sprint and remains the man to beat whenever the race comes back together in a straight fight.
Consistently in the mix in the bunch sprints and dangerous if the wind picks up and echelons form.
Podiumed behind Merlier in Bordeaux and has a well-drilled leadout for a straight fight to the line.

days.headline: The longest stage of the race skirts the edge of the Jura to Belfort — distance plus constant rolling terrain makes this a strongman's breakaway day.

picks.why: A durable puncheur who can survive a long day out front in the escape.
Loves a long, rolling breakaway stage exactly like this one.
A classics powerhouse capable of forcing the winning move on a long, punchy stage.

days.headline: A dense Vosges mountain stage into Le Markstein Fellering — the gradients aren't Alpine but the climbs come relentlessly, punishing any off day.

picks.why: Won the mountain finish at Le Lioran and is the rider best placed to exploit a repeatedly punchy finale like this.
The clearest GC threat to Pogačar so far and a rider who thrives when climbs come back-to-back rather than in one long haul.
The form of the Tour's breakout young climber suits a day where repeated steep pitches matter more than raw altitude.

days.headline: Into the Alps at last, capped by a savage, little-used summit finish at the Plateau de Solaison — with a rest day to follow, expect the GC favourites to empty the tank.

picks.why: The dominant climber of the race so far and the rider most able to press an advantage on a summit this steep.
The rider with the best track record of matching Pogačar on sustained high-mountain climbs, with a rest day cushion afterward to recover.
Has stayed glued to the front of the GC group in the mountains all race and is a threat to move on the toughest summit finish yet.

days.headline: The race against the clock along the shore of Lake Geneva from Évian to Thonon — flat, fast, pure power-TT terrain.

picks.why: A former world time-trial champion and the strongest pure specialist against the watch in the field.
Loses very little to the dedicated TT men and could extend his GC lead further on a course this fast.
A recognised TT specialist who can post a top result even outside the GC picture.

fantasy.strategy: The stage 8-14 transfer window closes with the Le Markstein mountain finale, so use any remaining transfers now to lock in a durable breakaway pick for Belfort alongside a core climber for the Vosges — climbing stages outscore sprint days in this format. Keep a pure sprinter (Merlier remains the standout) in the squad for the flat run to Chalon-sur-Saône rather than paying a premium for climbers who'll sit idle that day. Save the double-points bonus stage and fresh transfers for the stage 15-21 window, which opens with the brutal Plateau de Solaison summit finish and also contains the Évian-Thonon time trial — a good moment to add a TT specialist alongside the GC climbers before the Alps take full control of the race.
